text.fullText: Re: Opposition to Proposed Rule Amending Clean Water Act Section 401​ Submitted February 16, 2026 Dear Administrator Zeldin: On behalf of the Sierra Club Florida Chapter, representing thousands of members who live, work, and recreate across Florida, we submit these comments in strong opposition to the proposed amendments to Clean Water Act Section 401. Our members depend directly on clean rivers, springs, wetlands, and drinking water sources for their health, livelihoods, and quality of life, and any weakening of Florida’s authority to protect these waters causes immediate and lasting harm to the communities we represent. We write to strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed rule, published on January 13, 2026, that would amend regulations implementing Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA). This proposal would significantly weaken the ability of states, Tribes, and local governments to protect their rivers, lakes, wetlands, and drinking water sources from pollution associated with federally permitted or licensed projects, including pipelines, dams, industrial facilities, and data centers. Section 401 is a cornerstone of the Clean Water Act’s cooperative federalism framework. Congress deliberately designed this provision to ensure that federal permitting decisions do not override local and regional water quality protections. By empowering states and Tribes to review and condition federal permits to ensure compliance with water quality standards and other water-related requirements, Section 401 serves as one of the most effective safeguards for community waters nationwide. The proposed rule largely revives and repackages the 2020 Section 401 rule, which sharply curtailed state and Tribal authority after nearly fifty years of consistent practice and interpretation. That rule was widely criticized, legally vulnerable, and ultimately corrected by EPA’s 2023 rule, which restored the appropriate balance envisioned by Congress while providing clarity, reasonable timelines, and procedural certainty for permit applicants. Of particular concern, the proposed rule unlawfully narrows the scope of Section 401 review from the full activity subject to federal permitting to only the “discharge from a point source.” This interpretation conflicts with the plain language of the Clean Water Act and longstanding Supreme Court precedent. By preventing states and Tribes from evaluating the totality of a project’s impacts on water quality, this change would undermine their ability to protect stream flows, aquatic habitat, designated uses such as fishing and recreation, and downstream drinking water supplies. Beyond this fundamental flaw, the proposed rule further erodes state and Tribal authority by restricting access to necessary information, granting applicants an effective veto over protective conditions, imposing rigid timelines that force rushed decisions, weakening Tribal authority, and narrowing the definition of “water quality requirements” in ways that exclude critical protections such as minimum flows needed for ecological health. Florida-Specific Impacts: Outstanding Springs, Rivers, and BMAP Programs These changes would be especially harmful in Florida, where water protection depends heavily on Outstanding Florida Springs, Outstanding Florida Waters, and the state’s Basin Management Action Plan (BMAP) programs. Florida’s waters are uniquely vulnerable due to the state’s highly permeable karst limestone geology, which allows pollutants to move rapidly from the land surface into groundwater, springs, rivers, and drinking water supplies with little natural filtration. Because Florida’s surface waters and groundwater are so closely connected, impacts from federally permitted projects often extend far beyond a single discharge point. Section 401 currently allows Florida to evaluate the entire project activity and its cumulative impacts within sensitive springsheds and watersheds. The proposed rule would prevent the state from addressing many of the very impacts that threaten Florida’s waters, including increased nutrient pollution, reduced spring flows, sinkhole risks, and long-term degradation of aquifer-fed rivers. Florida’s BMAP programs were created to restore impaired springs and rivers by reducing nutrient pollution over time. These plans rely on the state’s ability to prevent new or expanded projects from undermining restoration goals. Weakening Section 401 would allow federally permitted projects to proceed even when they conflict with BMAP nutrient reduction targets, restoration timelines, and minimum flow protections—effectively forcing Florida to clean up pollution while losing the authority to stop new harm. This creates a deeply flawed outcome: Florida remains legally obligated to restore Outstanding Springs and Rivers, yet is stripped of one of its most important tools to ensure new federally permitted projects do not worsen already-impaired waters. The proposed rule is particularly troubling given the ongoing reduction in federal Clean Water Act jurisdiction over wetlands and streams. As fewer waters receive federal protection, Section 401 becomes more critical—not less—as one of the last remaining mechanisms for states to safeguard water quality. Weakening it now will disproportionately harm Florida communities, increase long-term cleanup costs, threaten drinking water supplies, and accelerate the loss of globally unique spring ecosystems. Conclusion EPA’s own analysis shows that Section 401 rarely delays or denies projects and is not an obstacle to responsible development. The proposed rule does not address a real problem. Instead, it prioritizes expedited permitting over clean water, public health, and the rights of states and Tribes to protect their own resources. For Florida, this proposal is not an abstract regulatory change—it is a direct threat to Outstanding Florida Springs, Outstanding Florida Waters, and the success of BMAP restoration efforts. The proposed rule would allow federally permitted projects to move forward even when they jeopardize spring flows, increase nutrient pollution, or undermine long-term restoration goals. For these reasons, we urge the Environmental Protection Agency to withdraw this proposed rule and retain the 2023 Section 401 regulations, which appropriately balance regulatory clarity with the Clean Water Act’s fundamental purpose: to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters. Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this critically important issue. Sincerely,​ Sierra Club Florida Chapter​ Florida Authorities Referenced ●​ Florida Springs and Aquifer Protection Act, Fla. Stat. §§ 373.801–373.813 ●​ Total Maximum Daily Loads & Basin Management Action Plans, Fla. Stat. § 403.067 ●​ Outstanding Florida Waters, Fla. Admin. Code R. 62-302.700 ●​ Minimum Flows and Levels, Fla. Stat. § 373.042
